calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
Off Calendar Aug-28-2020 Off Calendar

Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate
Off Calendar Aug-28-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Albert Garcia, Esq. 510-848-5190 ajgmedlaw@yahoo.com for Petitioner.
Megan O'Carroll, Deputy Attorney General, 916-210-7543 for Respondent.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Richard Sauer's petition for writ of administrative mandate is denied.

Following hearings, respondent California Medical Board placed Dr. Sauer's medical license on probation for five years - to run concurrently with probation from another case. (Dec. p. 17.)

Dr. Sauer's writ memorandum argues that the "Board did not proceed in the manner required by law." He complains that, while he was not found incompetent, a Board finding employs "the language of 'incompetence'" by using the phrase "general fitness to practice." However, the Board's decision clearly states that Sauer's conduct was "negligence" (see, e.g., id. at pp. 18-19); no due process violation occurred. So too with Sauer's complaint that he paid the hearing reporter for a transcript.

Dr. Sauer's memo also argues that the "Board's findings are unsupported by the weight of the evidence." The parties agree that independent judgment is the standard of review, which is "synonymous with the preponderance of the evidence standard." (Ettinger v. Board of Medical Quality Assurance (1982) 133 Cal.App.3d 853.) As discussed below, I find the Board's findings to be supported by the evidence.

Based largely on expert testimony from Dr. Shing Lui, the Board found that Dr. Sauer:

-"failed to perform an adequate initial neurological consultation" on the patient by not including in his report, any "past medical history of allergies...alcohol and drug use...or vital signs" save blood pressure. (Dec. p. 11; II RT 32-33; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer pits his own testimony against Dr. Lui's. Notably, however, Lui was qualified at hearing to testify as an expert, while Sauer was not. (Sauer failed to timely designate an expert.)

-"failed to legibly and accurately document relevant medical information." (Dec. pp. 11-12; II RT 33-34; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer effectively conceded this point. (III RT 13.)

-ordered "the transcranial Doppler test without medical indications." (Dec. p. 12; II RT 35-36, 39-41; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer again pits his personal views against Dr. Lui's expert opinions.

-in "the most serious issue," "prescribed Keppra to [the] patient with a history of depression and suicide." (Dec. p. 13: II RT 41-44; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer claims a "difference of medical opinion" - an argument that would have had more force had he actually introduced expert opinions at hearing.

-"failed to effectively communicate with" the patient "regarding her treatment." (Dec. pp. 13-14: II RT 44-45; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer labels this "highly subjective;" Dr. Lui disagrees.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
